title: Foreign
subtitle: International cinema, organized by accessibility. Thriller-leaning and structurally rigorous; less deep arthouse.

sections.name: I. High Priority — International Thriller
sections.desc: Direct fits given your structural-rigor + cons + crime film loves. The international tradition closest to what you've already loved.

items.title: Parasite
items.whyPriority: First non-English film to win Best Picture. Class warfare via long con. Direct fit for your Cons & Courtroom + thriller pillars.
items.pitch: A poor family schemes to become servants of a wealthy family. The film changes shape three times.

critics.quote: Won 4 Oscars including Best Picture, Director, Original Screenplay, International Feature.

items.title: Memories of Murder
items.whyPriority: Bong's earlier masterpiece. True-crime serial killer investigation procedural. Considered by Korean cinema scholars his greatest film.
items.pitch: Two rural detectives investigate Korea's first serial killings (1986-91). The case was unsolved for 30 years.

critics.quote: Routinely cited among the greatest South Korean films and procedural crime films ever made.

items.title: Tell No One
items.whyPriority: Harlan Coben adaptation. Tightly plotted thriller. The 8-minute foot chase is one of the great chase sequences. Direct fit for your twist-architecture pillar.
items.pitch: Eight years after his wife's murder, a Parisian doctor receives an email that suggests she's still alive.

critics.quote: Won 4 Césars including Best Director.

items.title: The Secret in Their Eyes
items.whyPriority: Won Best Foreign Film. Argentine retired investigator returns to a 25-year-old unsolved case. Direct fit for thriller + structural rigor.
items.pitch: A retired Buenos Aires legal investigator writes a novel about a case that haunted him for decades.

critics.quote: Won Best Foreign Language Film.

items.title: Z
items.whyPriority: Political thriller masterpiece. Loosely fictionalized Greek assassination. Won Best Foreign Film and Best Editing at the Oscars.
items.pitch: A leftist politician is assassinated; an investigating magistrate uncovers a state conspiracy.

critics.quote: Won 2 Oscars. Five nominations including Best Picture — rare for a foreign film at the time.

items.title: The Wages of Fear
items.whyPriority: Considered one of the most suspenseful films ever made. Four desperate men drive trucks of nitroglycerin across South American mountains.
items.pitch: Four destitute Europeans in South America take a near-suicidal job: drive trucks of unstable explosives over treacherous roads.

critics.quote: Won the Palme d'Or and BAFTA Best Film.

items.title: Diabolique
items.whyPriority: Clouzot's other masterpiece. The structural template Hitchcock himself studied. Twist-driven thriller.
items.pitch: A boarding-school headmaster's wife and mistress conspire to murder him. The body disappears.

critics.quote: Hitchcock attempted to buy the rights and was outbid; he then bought Psycho's source novel out of frustration.

items.title: Le Samouraï
items.whyPriority: Alain Delon as a Parisian hitman. Influential on virtually every cool-killer film since (Drive, Ghost Dog, Léon, John Wick all cite it).
items.pitch: A Parisian contract killer tries to evade police after being seen at the scene of his latest hit.

critics.quote: Routinely cited among the most influential French films of the 1960s.

items.title: A Prophet
items.whyPriority: Cannes Grand Prix 2009. Young Arab inmate rises in the French prison hierarchy. Direct fit for your crime + structure pillar.
items.pitch: A 19-year-old French Arab is sentenced to six years in prison. He educates himself, gains protectors, and rises within the system.

critics.quote: Grand Prix. Nine Césars including Best Film.

items.title: Headhunters
items.whyPriority: Jo Nesbø adaptation. Twisty corporate-espionage thriller from director who later made The Imitation Game.
items.pitch: A Norwegian executive recruiter steals art from his clients to support a lifestyle beyond his means. He picks the wrong target.

critics.quote: Norway's biggest box office hit at the time. Considered the strongest Nesbø adaptation.

items.title: Burning
items.whyPriority: Murakami short-story adaptation. Slow-burn psychological thriller. Tied with Memories of Murder among critics' top Korean films of the 2010s.
items.pitch: A young aspiring writer in Seoul reconnects with a childhood friend who introduces him to her wealthy new boyfriend.

critics.quote: FIPRESCI Prize. Highest score in Cannes Critics' history at the time.

items.title: Oldboy
items.whyPriority: Park's revenge masterpiece. The hallway hammer-fight sequence is among the most influential action setpieces ever filmed. Twist ending is foundational.
items.pitch: A Seoul businessman is imprisoned in a hotel-room cell for 15 years without explanation. Then he's released.

critics.quote: Grand Prix from a jury chaired by Quentin Tarantino.

items.title: The Handmaiden
items.whyPriority: Park's Sarah Waters adaptation. Korean colonial-era con-artist plot with three perspective shifts. Direct fit for your cons + structural-rigor pillar.
items.pitch: A Korean conwoman is hired as handmaiden to a wealthy Japanese heiress. She and her partner plan to steal the heiress's fortune.

critics.quote: BAFTA Best Foreign Film. Considered Park's most accomplished post-Oldboy work.

sections.name: II. Medium Priority — Foundational International
sections.desc: Major canonical works that fit your sensibility without being deep arthouse.

items.title: The Rules of the Game
items.whyPriority: Routinely listed among the greatest films ever made. Sight & Sound poll mainstay. Pre-war French society laid bare.
items.pitch: Aristocrats and servants gather at a country estate. Affairs, hunts, secrets, deaths.

critics.quote: #11 in Greatest Films of All Time poll.

items.title: The 400 Blows
items.pitch: French New Wave's most accessible founding text. Truffaut's autobiographical first feature.

critics.quote: Won Best Director.

items.title: Breathless
items.pitch: Godard's debut. The film that broke film grammar — jump cuts, casual delivery, hand-held camera.

critics.quote: Routinely cited as among the most influential debuts in film history.

items.title: Bicycle Thieves
items.pitch: Italian Neorealism founding text. A Roman father and son search for a stolen bicycle that he needs for work.

critics.quote: Honorary Oscar before the Foreign Film category existed.

items.title: 8½
items.pitch: Fellini's most personal film about a director's creative paralysis.

critics.quote: Top-30 Greatest Films of All Time.

items.title: Seven Samurai
items.pitch: Kurosawa's masterpiece. A village hires seven samurai to defend against bandits. Direct ancestor of nearly every team-assembly action film.

critics.quote: Top-25 Greatest Films of All Time.

items.title: Yojimbo
items.pitch: Kurosawa's most playful samurai film. Mifune as a wandering ronin who plays two warring factions against each other. Remade as A Fistful of Dollars.

critics.quote: Considered one of Kurosawa's most accessible works.

items.title: Ran
items.pitch: Kurosawa's King Lear. Visually astonishing late-career masterpiece.

critics.quote: Best Costume Oscar. Routinely cited as one of the great late-career works in cinema.

items.title: City of God
items.pitch: Rio de Janeiro favela crime drama spanning 20 years. The Goodfellas of Brazilian cinema.

critics.quote: Four Oscar nominations including Best Director.

items.title: Y Tu Mamá También
items.pitch: Cuarón's road movie. Two teenage boys and an older woman drive across Mexico to a beach.

critics.quote: Oscar-nominated for Original Screenplay. Established Cuarón globally.

items.title: Pan's Labyrinth
items.pitch: Cross-listed in Fantasy. 1944 fascist Spain. A young girl encounters a mythological labyrinth.

critics.quote: Won 3 Oscars. Six nominations including Best Foreign Language Film.

sections.name: III. Worth Considering
sections.desc: Adjacent territory. Festival prestige fits less squarely with your loves but still rewarding.

items.title: Drive My Car
items.pitch: Murakami adaptation. Slow, contemplative, theater-driven. Tonally meditative but structurally rigorous.

critics.quote: Won Best International Feature. Best Picture nominee.

items.title: Roma
items.pitch: Cuarón's autobiographical film about the housekeeper who raised him in 1970s Mexico City. Black-and-white, 65mm.

critics.quote: Won 3 Oscars including Best Director and Best Foreign Language Film.

items.title: Cold War
items.pitch: Black-and-white tragic romance across post-WWII Poland and Paris. 89 minutes, every shot composed.

critics.quote: Best Director. Three Oscar nominations.

items.title: Ida
items.pitch: Pawlikowski's earlier black-and-white masterpiece. A young Polish nun discovers she is Jewish on the eve of taking her vows.

critics.quote: Won Best Foreign Language Film.

items.title: The Lives of Others
items.pitch: Cross-listed in Spy. East German Stasi officer surveils a playwright. Direct fit for spy + drama.

critics.quote: Won Best Foreign Language Film.

items.title: Amour
items.pitch: Haneke's most humane film. An elderly Parisian couple's marriage in its final stage.

critics.quote: Palme d'Or. Won Best Foreign Language Film.

items.title: Tokyo Story
items.pitch: Routinely listed among the greatest films ever made. Aging parents visit their grown children in Tokyo.

critics.quote: Top-5 Greatest Films of All Time.

items.title: In the Mood for Love
items.pitch: Hong Kong romance. Two neighbors in 1962 Hong Kong realize their spouses are having an affair with each other.

critics.quote: #5 Greatest Film of All Time.

sections.name: IV. Auteur Filmographies
sections.desc: Primary genre home for director-specific works appearing in the Auteur tab. Organized by director.

items.title: The Seventh Seal
items.whyPriority: Best entry point. Knight + Death + chess + plague.
items.pitch: A medieval knight returning from the Crusades challenges Death to a game of chess as the Black Plague sweeps Sweden — buying time to do one meaningful act before he dies.

critics.quote: Special Jury Prize.

items.title: Wild Strawberries
items.whyPriority: His most accessible great film. Aging professor confronts his life.
items.pitch: An aging professor, driving to receive an honorary degree, is overtaken by memories and dreams of his life and its failures. Bergman's warmest film.

critics.quote: Berlin Golden Bear.

items.title: Persona
items.whyPriority: Bergman at his most experimental. Two women — actress and nurse — merge identities. The film school masterclass.
items.pitch: An actress stops speaking. Her nurse cares for her at a remote cottage. The boundary between them dissolves.

critics.quote: Routinely cited as Bergman's masterpiece. Sight & Sound canonical.

items.title: Cries and Whispers
items.pitch: Three sisters at a country estate as one of them dies. Bergman's most painful film.

critics.quote: Five Oscar nominations including Best Picture, Best Director.

items.title: Fanny and Alexander
items.whyPriority: Bergman's farewell. Warmer than most of his catalog. Watch the 312-min TV version if you commit.
items.pitch: An early-1900s Swedish family across years. Wealth, marriage, theater, magic, religion.

critics.quote: Won 4 Oscars including Best Foreign Language Film, Cinematography, Art Direction, Costume Design.

items.title: Scenes from a Marriage
items.pitch: A marriage across years, fights, separation, return. The TV version is the one to watch.

critics.quote: Cited by many as the most honest depiction of marriage ever filmed.

items.title: Winter Light
items.pitch: A pastor losing his faith during one Sunday's services. Sparse, brutal.

items.title: Through a Glass Darkly
items.pitch: A young woman recovering from mental illness during a family vacation. Won Best Foreign Language Film.

critics.quote: Won Best Foreign Language Film.

items.title: Rashomon
items.whyPriority: His international breakthrough. The film that gave us the word 'rashomon' for contradictory testimonies.
items.pitch: A samurai's murder in feudal Japan is recounted by four witnesses, each giving a contradictory account. The foundational film on unreliable narration.

critics.quote: Won Golden Lion at Venice.

items.title: Ikiru
items.whyPriority: A bureaucrat with cancer tries to do one meaningful thing before dying. Many call it Kurosawa's most emotional film.
items.pitch: An aging Tokyo civil servant, told he has months to live, tries to make his life mean something.

critics.quote: Routinely cited among Kurosawa's masterpieces. Watanabe Takashi's central performance is iconic.

items.title: Sanjuro
items.pitch: Yojimbo sequel. Lighter, looser. The blood-spurt at the climax has been imitated for 60 years.

items.title: The Hidden Fortress
items.pitch: Adventure film told from peasants' perspective. Lucas cited it as a structural model for Star Wars — R2-D2 and C-3PO are direct descendants.

critics.quote: Berlin Silver Bear for Best Director.

items.title: High and Low
items.pitch: A wealthy executive's chauffeur's son is mistakenly kidnapped. Shifts from boardroom thriller to cops procedural.

critics.quote: Routinely cited as one of Kurosawa's greatest non-period films.

items.title: La Strada
items.whyPriority: Fellini's most heartbreaking. Giulietta Masina as a simple-minded young woman sold to a brutish strongman. Won Best Foreign Language Film.
items.pitch: Postwar Italy. A traveling strongman buys a young woman from her impoverished mother. Their road life.

critics.quote: Won Best Foreign Language Film.

items.title: Nights of Cabiria
items.pitch: Masina as a Roman prostitute who keeps believing in love. The final shot is one of cinema's most famous.

critics.quote: Won Best Foreign Language Film.

items.title: La Dolce Vita
items.whyPriority: Marcello Mastroianni drifting through a week of Roman society. The word 'paparazzi' came from a character's name.
items.pitch: A gossip journalist drifts through seven nights of Roman parties, encounters, and emptiness.

critics.quote: Palme d'Or.

items.title: Amarcord
items.pitch: Fellini's most autobiographical. A year in 1930s Rimini under Mussolini. Comic, nostalgic, sad.

critics.quote: Won Best Foreign Language Film.

items.title: Andrei Rublev
items.whyPriority: His masterpiece. Three-and-a-half hours.
items.pitch: The life of the great Russian icon painter across four decades of medieval Russia — invasion, famine, and spiritual doubt. Tarkovsky's most expansive film.

items.title: Mirror
items.whyPriority: His most personal and most difficult. Often cited as his greatest film by other directors.
items.pitch: A dying poet's memories of childhood, his mother, war, and Russia.

critics.quote: Frequently named the greatest film ever made by other filmmakers.

items.title: Nostalghia
items.pitch: A Russian poet researches an exile in Italy. His own homesickness becomes the film's spiritual subject.

critics.quote: Tarkovsky shared the Best Director prize.

items.title: The Sacrifice
items.pitch: Tarkovsky's final film. Made while he was dying. A man bargains with God to prevent nuclear war.

critics.quote: Grand Prix and Critics' Prize.

items.title: Incendies
items.pitch: Twins travel to the Middle East to investigate their dead mother's past. Brutal, structurally devastating.

critics.quote: Best Foreign Language Film nomination.
